mappings.rationale: Section A.1 covers the complete history of astronomy from ancient time reckoning through 20th-century radio astronomy and black holes.
Section A.2 covers the history of physics from Greek speculations through 20th-century relativity, quantum theory, and condensed-matter physics.
Section A.3 covers the history of chemistry from alchemy through 20th-century instrumental methods and quantum chemistry.
Section C covers the nature and scope of physics including mechanics, thermodynamics, electricity, magnetism, optics, and quantum mechanics.
Section D covers the nature and scope of chemistry including composition, properties, and transformations of matter.
Section D.2 lists physical chemistry as a subdivision, and A.3.c.vi covers electrochemistry development.
Section A.3.c.v covers the development of organic chemistry including valence, aromatic compounds, and structural theory.
Section D.2 lists biochemistry as a subdivision of chemistry.
Section A.3.c.i specifically covers the development of the periodic table by Mendeleyev and Meyer.
Section A.3.c.ii covers discoveries of new elements and isotopes, and A.3.c.i the periodic table.
Section A.3.c.iv covers the development of theories of molecular structure and chemical reaction.
Section A.3.c.ii mentions the discovery of isotopes as part of 19th-century chemistry.
Section A.2.d.iv covers the discovery of radioactivity and X-rays, and A.3.c.ii covers radioactive elements.
Section B.5 covers cosmology and cosmogony, and A.1.f.iv covers relativistic cosmologies including the big bang model.
Section B.5 directly covers astrophysics including the study of stars, galaxies, and the universe.
Section A.1.f.i-ii covers statistical studies of stars and theories of stellar structure, formation, and evolution.
Section A.1.f.i covers statistical studies of galaxies and B.5 covers astrophysics including galaxy studies.
Section A.1.f.vii specifically mentions identification of possible black holes as a 20th-century development.
Section A.1.f.v covers exploration of planets and their satellites, and B.2 lists planetary sciences.
Section A.1.f.v covers exploration of planets and their satellites with unmanned probes.
The Sun is central to solar-system astronomy (A.1.f.v) and is a key subject in astrophysics (B.5).
Section B.2 covers planetary and lunar sciences and the origin of the solar system.
Section A.1.c.iii covers the invention and use of telescopes, and A.1.f.viii covers advances in instrumentation.
Section A.2.e.i covers the development of relativity theory, and A.1.f.iii covers astronomical tests of general relativity.
Section A.2.e.ii covers the development of quantum theory, wave mechanics, and statistical mechanics.
Section A.2.e.iii covers theories of atomic structure, nuclear interactions, and elementary particles.
Section A.2.e.iii covers nuclear interactions and C.2 lists nuclear physics as a component discipline.
Section A.2.d.iii covers the development of thermodynamics including the laws and kinetic theory of gases.
Section A.2.d.ii covers the development of theories of electricity, magnetism, and electromagnetic waves.
Section A.2.d.i covers wave vs. corpuscular theory of light, and A.2.e.v mentions nonlinear optics and lasers.
Section C.2 lists sound as a component discipline of physics.
Wave phenomena are central to optics, acoustics, and electromagnetic theory in A.2.d and C.2.
Section A.2.e.iv specifically covers the development of condensed-matter physics and its contributions to electronics.
Superconductivity is part of condensed-matter physics (A.2.e.iv) and its contributions to electronics.
Section C.1 defines physics as concerned with matter and energy, and A.2.d.iv covers the atomic theory of matter.
Section A.1.c.iv covers universal gravitation, and A.1.f.iii covers gravitational effects in relativity.
Crystallography relates to the study of crystal structure, part of the methods of chemistry (D.3) and condensed-matter physics.
Water is a fundamental substance studied through physical chemistry and molecular structure, topics in D.
Section A.1.c covers Galileo's era including heliocentric systems and the telescope, and A.2.c covers physics contributions.
Section A.1.c.iv covers Newton's theory of universal gravitation and A.2.c covers mechanics and optics contributions.
Section A.1.c.i covers the geocentric and heliocentric world systems, Copernicus's central contribution.
Section A.2.d.ii covers theories of electricity, magnetism, and electromagnetic waves, to which Faraday was a primary contributor.
Section A.2.e.ii covers the development of quantum theory, to which Bohr made foundational contributions.
Section C.5 specifically lists geophysics as an interdisciplinary field of physics.
Fluid mechanics is a core topic within physics (C.2, mechanics).
Soft matter is a branch of condensed-matter physics covered in A.2.e.iv.
Materials science connects to condensed-matter physics (A.2.e.iv) and the study of matter (D.1).
Entropy is central to thermodynamics (A.2.d.iii) and the laws of thermodynamics in physics (C.2).
Section C.6 covers philosophical problems in physics at the formal, quantum, macrophysical, and cosmological levels.
Nanotechnology derives from condensed-matter physics (A.2.e.iv) and relates to interdisciplinary applications (C.5).
Astrobiology connects astronomy (B) with biology, relating to the interdisciplinary fields of physical science.
The concept of vacuum and nothing relates to fundamental questions about matter, energy, and space in physics (C).
Nuclear power applies nuclear physics covered in A.2.e.iii on nuclear interactions and energy.
Nuclear weapons derive from nuclear physics in A.2.e.iii on nuclear interactions and elementary particles.
Mineralogy connects to crystallography and chemistry, relating to composition and properties of matter in D.
Section A.2.d.iii covers theories of heat, and A.3.b.i covers combustion chemistry (phlogiston, Lavoisier).
